title: Battle of Clastidium
notes: The Battle of Clastidium was fought in 222 BC between a Roman army led by the consul Marcus Claudius Marcellus and a Celtic force of the transalpine Gaesatae, called by the cisalpine Insubres. The Gaesatae were led by Viridomarus (Florus) or Britomartus (Plutarch), as the name varies in translation. The Romans won the battle, and in the process, Marcellus earned the spolia opima, one of the highest honors in ancient Rome, by killing the king in single combat. It was also notable as one of the few battles won by the Roman cavalry without any aid by the legions.

title: Battle of Emesa
notes: The Battle of Emesa was fought in 272 between the Roman armies led by their emperor Aurelian and the Palmyrene forces led by their empress, Zenobia and general Zabdas.

title: Secular Games
notes: The Secular or Saecular Games was an ancient Roman religious celebration involving sacrifices, theatrical performances, and public games. It was held irregularly in Rome for three days and nights to mark the ends of various eras and to celebrate the beginning of the next. In particular, the Romans reckoned a saeculum as the longest possible length of human life, either 100 or 110 years in length; as such, it was used to mark various centennials, particularly anniversaries from the computed founding of Rome.

title: Battle of the Nile
notes: The Battle of the Nile in early 47 BC saw the combined Roman–Egyptian armies of Julius Caesar and Cleopatra VII defeat those of the rival Queen Arsinoe IV and King Ptolemy XIII and secure the throne of Egypt.

details.markdown: Julius Caesar's intervention on Cleopatra's behalf against her co-ruler siblings is the clearest direct link between Rome and Ptolemaic Egypt in our data -- it's the same event from both civilizations' side, not two separate battles.

title: Battle of Raphia
notes: The Battle of Raphia was fought on 22 June 217 BC near modern Rafah between the forces of Ptolemy IV Philopator, king and pharaoh of Ptolemaic Egypt and Antiochus III the Great of the Seleucid Empire during the Syrian Wars. It was one of the largest battles of the Hellenistic kingdoms and of the ancient world, and determined the sovereignty of Coele-Syria.

title: Expedition of Ptolemy II Philadelphus to the Black Sea
notes: Expedition of Ptolemy II Philadelphus to the Black Sea

title: Battle of Thebes
notes: 335 BCE battle between Alexander the Great and the Greek city state of Thebes

title: Battle of Kirina
notes: 1235 confrontation between the Sosso king Sumanguru Kanté and the Mandinka prince Sundiata Keita

title: Battle of Jenné
notes: 1599 battle between Morocco and the Mali Empire

title: Battle of Guadalete
notes: The Battle of Guadalete was the first major battle of the Muslim conquest of the Iberian Peninsula, fought in July 711 at an unidentified location in what is now southern Spain between the Visigoths under their king, Roderic, and the invading forces of the Umayyad Caliphate, composed of mainly Berbers and some Arabs under the commander Tariq ibn Ziyad. The battle was significant as the culmination of a series of Umayyad attacks and the beginning of al-Andalus. Roderic was killed in the battle, along with many members of the Visigothic nobility, opening the way for the capture of the Visigothic capital of Toledo.

title: Rebellion of the Seven States
notes: 154 BC rebellion against the Han dynasty of China

title: Battle of Baideng
notes: 200 BCE battle

title: Disasters of Partisan Prohibitions
notes: anti-eunuch protests by Chinese scholars and students (166 and 169)

title: Shunga coup d'état
notes: Shunga coup d'état

title: Battle of Cajamarca
notes: The Battle of Cajamarca, also spelled Cajamalca, was the ambush and seizure of the Incan ruler Atahualpa by a small Spanish force led by Francisco Pizarro, on November 16, 1532. The Spanish killed thousands of Atahualpa's counselors, commanders, and unarmed attendants in the great plaza of Cajamarca, and caused his armed host outside the town to flee. The capture of Atahualpa marked the opening stage of the conquest of the pre-Columbian civilization of Peru.

title: Chanka–Inca War
notes: The Inca-Chanka war was a semi-legendary, mytho-historical, potentially mythical, military conflict fought between Cusco and the Chanka chiefdom, several generations prior to the arrival of Europeans. It is the final conflict between these two people.

title: Aksum begins minting gold coinage
notes: King Endubis issues Aksum's own gold coins, one of only four ancient states (with Rome, Persia, and the Kushan Empire) to do so -- a sign of its trading reach.

metadata.curationReason: Wikidata P17/P361/P710 relational query returned too few results for this civilization

title: Ezana converts to Christianity
notes: King Ezana adopts Christianity, making Aksum one of the first states in the world to do so and reorienting it toward the Byzantine world.

metadata.curationReason: Wikidata P17/P361/P710 relational query returned too few results for this civilization

title: Ezana's conquest of Kush
notes: Ezana leads a military campaign that destroys the already-declining Kingdom of Kush at Meroe, ending Kushite power on the Nile.

metadata.curationReason: Wikidata P17/P361/P710 relational query returned too few results for this civilization

title: Kaleb's invasion of Himyar
notes: King Kaleb invades Himyar across the Red Sea to depose Dhu Nuwas, who had been persecuting Christians there, briefly extending Aksumite control into South Arabia.

metadata.curationReason: Wikidata P17/P361/P710 relational query returned too few results for this civilization

title: Year of the Elephant
notes: Aksum's viceroy in Yemen, Abraha, marches on Mecca with an army said to include a war elephant. In Islamic tradition the campaign fails and the year becomes a fixed reference point -- the year of Muhammad's birth.

metadata.curationReason: Wikidata P17/P361/P710 relational query returned too few results for this civilization

title: Decline of Aksum
notes: Aksum's power collapses amid trade decline and, per tradition, an invasion led by a queen remembered as Gudit, who is said to have sacked the capital and burned its churches.

metadata.curationReason: Wikidata P17/P361/P710 relational query returned too few results for this civilization

title: Mansa Musa's hajj
notes: Mansa Musa travels to Mecca with a caravan reportedly carrying tons of gold, spending so freely in Cairo that he depresses the regional gold price for years -- the event that put Mali on European maps.

metadata.curationReason: Wikidata P17/P361/P710 relational query returned too few results for this civilization

title: Sankore Madrasah founded
notes: Under Mansa Musa's patronage, Timbuktu's Sankore mosque develops into a major center of Islamic scholarship, part of what becomes the University of Sankore.

metadata.curationReason: Wikidata P17/P361/P710 relational query returned too few results for this civilization

title: Ibn Battuta visits Mali
notes: The Moroccan traveler Ibn Battuta journeys to the Mali Empire and records a detailed eyewitness account of its court, justice system, and wealth.

metadata.curationReason: Wikidata P17/P361/P710 relational query returned too few results for this civilization

title: Fall of Timbuktu to Songhai
notes: Sunni Ali of the rising Songhai Empire captures Timbuktu from Mali, a major step in Mali's long decline as Songhai supplants it as the region's dominant power.

metadata.curationReason: Wikidata P17/P361/P710 relational query returned too few results for this civilization

title: Chandragupta founds the Maurya Empire
notes: Chandragupta Maurya overthrows the Nanda dynasty and establishes the Maurya Empire, which will grow to unify most of the Indian subcontinent.

metadata.curationReason: Wikidata P17/P361/P710 relational query returned too few results for this civilization

title: Seleucid-Maurya War
notes: Chandragupta fights Seleucus I Nicator, a successor of Alexander the Great; the resulting treaty cedes territory in the northwest to the Mauryas in exchange for 500 war elephants.

metadata.curationReason: Wikidata P17/P361/P710 relational query returned too few results for this civilization

title: Ashoka becomes emperor
notes: Ashoka, grandson of Chandragupta, takes the throne. His reign will become the empire's high point and its most consequential turn.

metadata.curationReason: Wikidata P17/P361/P710 relational query returned too few results for this civilization

title: Kalinga War
notes: Ashoka's brutal conquest of Kalinga leaves an estimated hundred thousand dead. Appalled by the carnage, he renounces further conquest and turns to Buddhism -- the empire's defining pivot.

metadata.curationReason: Wikidata P17/P361/P710 relational query returned too few results for this civilization

title: Ashoka's edicts inscribed
notes: Ashoka has edicts on Buddhist ethics and governance carved into rocks and pillars across the empire, in multiple scripts and languages -- among the oldest deciphered inscriptions in India.

metadata.curationReason: Wikidata P17/P361/P710 relational query returned too few results for this civilization

title: The Twelve Tables
notes: Rome's first written law code, posted in the Forum after plebeian pressure for legal transparency. The foundation of later Roman law.

title: Colosseum completed
notes: The Flavian Amphitheatre opens under Emperor Titus with a hundred days of games, becoming Rome's largest and most enduring public monument.

title: Edict of Caracalla
notes: Emperor Caracalla grants Roman citizenship to nearly all free inhabitants of the empire, dissolving the old distinction between citizen and subject.

title: Zhang Qian's mission west
notes: Emperor Wu sends the envoy Zhang Qian to seek allies against the Xiongnu. He returns 13 years later with the first detailed Chinese account of Central Asia, opening the route that becomes the Silk Road.

title: Cai Lun improves papermaking
notes: Court official Cai Lun refines a cheap, standardized papermaking process using bark, hemp, and rags -- a technology that will eventually spread west through the Islamic world to Europe.

title: Records of the Grand Historian completed
notes: Sima Qian completes his sweeping history of China from legendary times to his own -- the model for all later Chinese dynastic histories.

title: Pachacuti founds the Inca Empire
notes: After defeating the Chanka, Pachacuti takes the throne and transforms Cusco from a regional chiefdom into an expanding imperial state.

title: Qhapaq Nan road network built
notes: The Inca build a road system eventually spanning some 40,000 km across the Andes, with rope bridges, waystations, and relay runners connecting the empire.

title: Machu Picchu built
notes: An estate is built high in the Andes for Pachacuti, likely as a royal retreat -- abandoned a century later and never found by the Spanish.

title: Mit'a labor system formalized
notes: The Inca state formalizes mit'a, a rotational labor tax used instead of money to build roads, temples, and terraces across the empire.

title: Philip II reforms the Macedonian army
notes: Philip II professionalizes the Macedonian army and introduces the sarissa-armed phalanx, laying the military foundation his son Alexander will use to conquer Persia.

title: League of Corinth formed
notes: Philip II organizes the Greek city-states (except Sparta) into a league under Macedonian leadership, ending centuries of Greek city-state independence.

title: Aristotle tutors Alexander
notes: Philip II hires Aristotle to tutor the young Alexander at Mieza, a three-year education that shapes Alexander's lifelong interest in science, medicine, and philosophy.

title: Alexander founds Alexandria
notes: Alexander the Great founds a city on the Egyptian coast bearing his name. After his death it becomes the capital of the Ptolemaic dynasty founded by his general Ptolemy.

title: Library of Alexandria founded
notes: Ptolemy II establishes the great Library and Mouseion at Alexandria, drawing scholars from across the Greek world and aiming to collect all the world's texts.

title: Lighthouse of Alexandria completed
notes: The Pharos of Alexandria, one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, is completed to guide ships into the harbor -- and stands for over a thousand years.

title: Rosetta Stone inscribed
notes: A priestly decree for Ptolemy V is inscribed in three scripts -- hieroglyphic, Demotic, and Greek. Rediscovered in 1799, it becomes the key that finally lets scholars read hieroglyphs.

title: Battle of Tours
notes: Charles Martel's Frankish army halts an Umayyad force near Tours, in what later European histories treat as the high-water mark of the Umayyad advance into Western Europe.

title: Dome of the Rock completed
notes: Caliph Abd al-Malik completes the Dome of the Rock in Jerusalem, one of the earliest and most enduring works of Islamic architecture.

title: Abd al-Malik's currency and administrative reforms
notes: Abd al-Malik introduces a standardized Islamic gold and silver coinage and makes Arabic the administrative language across the caliphate, replacing Greek and Persian bureaucratic systems.

title: Great Mosque of Damascus completed
notes: Caliph al-Walid I completes the Great Mosque of Damascus on the site of a former Byzantine church, a model for congregational mosques across the Islamic world.

title: Battle of Pydna
notes: Rome decisively defeats King Perseus of Macedon, ending the Antigonid dynasty and Macedon's independence -- the event behind civilization_macedon's own end date.

details.markdown: The Roman legion's flexibility famously exploited gaps that opened in the Macedonian phalanx on broken ground -- a tactical lesson later Roman writers returned to repeatedly. Macedon was carved into four Roman client republics afterward, then annexed outright in 148 BCE.
